title: Cloud Provider Path Visualization
dataSources: `index=thousandeyes_pathvis sourcetype=cisco:thousandeyes:path-vis` from the path visualization collection in `ta_cisco_thousandeyes` (Splunkbase 7719). Path visualization data provides the hop-by-hop network path between the ThousandEyes agent and the target — including intermediate router IPs, ASNs, and per-hop latency/loss. For cloud provider paths, this reveals the exact route traffic takes from your network into the cloud provider's backbone, through their internal network, and to the target service.
app: `ta_cisco_thousandeyes` — Cisco ThousandEyes App for Splunk ([Splunkbase 7719](https://splunkbase.splunk.com/app/7719)), Cisco Supported.
spl:
`path_viz_index` thousandeyes.test.name="*AWS*" OR thousandeyes.test.name="*Azure*" OR thousandeyes.test.name="*GCP*"
| stats avg(hop_count) as avg_hops max(hop_count) as max_hops dc(path_hash) as unique_paths by thousandeyes.source.agent.name, server.address
| where unique_paths > 1
| sort -unique_paths
description: Analyzes hop-by-hop path data for connections to cloud provider services, detecting path changes, unexpected routing through third-party networks, and per-hop latency contributions. Extends the basic path analysis (UC-5.9.5/5.9.6) to specifically focus on cloud-bound traffic, where path stability is critical for latency-sensitive workloads.
value: Traffic to cloud services traverses multiple networks and peering points. A path change from direct peering (your ISP → AWS) to transit (your ISP → Telia → NTT → AWS) can add 20–50 ms of latency. Cloud providers sometimes shift peering arrangements, reroute traffic through different PoPs, or experience backbone congestion — all invisible without path-level data. This UC detects when the path to your cloud services changes, quantifies the latency impact, and provides the AS-level detail needed to engage your ISP or cloud provider with evidence: "Our traffic to AWS us-east-1 is now routing through AS3356 (Lumen) instead of direct peering, adding 15 ms."
implementation: Uses the path visualization data collected by the same input that provides pathvis for UC-5.9.5. Filter by test names that target cloud provider endpoints.
visualization: (1) Path diagram (if rendered in ThousandEyes). (2) Table: cloud paths with hop count, unique paths, path stability indicator. (3) Timechart: hop count changes over time. (4) Alert indicator: paths with > 1 unique path in the measurement window.
knownFalsePositives:
**Cloud provider internal path changes.** Cloud providers (AWS, Azure, GCP) regularly optimize internal routing. Path changes within the cloud provider's AS are usually benign and don't affect latency. Only flag path changes that cross AS boundaries.

**ECMP load balancing.** Equal-Cost Multi-Path routing at ISP and cloud edges may show multiple paths simultaneously. This is normal and doesn't indicate instability. Check whether hop count and latency are consistent despite path variation.

**Traceroute-resistant hops.** Some cloud provider hops don't respond to ICMP/traceroute probes, showing as "*" or "unknown." This doesn't mean a problem — it's a security policy of the cloud provider.
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
• All prerequisites from UC-5.9.5 apply — the path visualization API polling input must be configured in `ta_cisco_thousandeyes`, delivering data to `thousandeyes_pathvis` via the `path_viz_index` macro.
• **Agent-to-Server tests targeting cloud-hosted services.** Tests must target endpoints hosted in cloud providers (AWS EC2/ELB, Azure VMs/App Services, GCP Compute/Cloud Run). Use a consistent naming convention (e.g., `SiteA-AWS-us-east-1`, `SiteA-Azure-westeurope`) so the SPL can filter cloud-bound tests from other tests. If naming conventions aren't practical, use ThousandEyes test tags or labels.
• **Enterprise Agents deployed at each source location.** Path visualization requires Enterprise Agents — Cloud Agents do not generate path data for API polling (though they do for in-UI viewing). Deploy agents at offices, data centers, and on-premises sites that connect to cloud services.
• **Understanding path visualization data model.** Path viz data is **API-polled, not HEC-pushed**. The `ta_cisco_thousandeyes` app polls the ThousandEyes Path Visualization API every 5–15 minutes (configurable). Each polling round returns hop-by-hop trace data: source → hop 1 → hop 2 → ... → destination. Each hop reports: `hop_ip` (IP address of the hop), `hop_prefix` (BGP prefix — identifies the network/ASN), `hop_latency` (per-hop latency), `hop_loss` (per-hop packet loss), `hop_count` (total hops to destination), and `path_hash` (a hash of the complete path — changes when the path changes).
• **Cloud provider IP ranges known.** AWS publishes IP ranges at `ip-ranges.amazonaws.com`, Azure at `download.microsoft.com`, and GCP at `www.gstatic.com/ipranges/cloud.json`. Knowing these ranges helps identify where in the path traffic enters the cloud provider's network — before that point is public internet (ISP responsibility), after that point is cloud provider (their backbone).

Step 1 — Configure data collection
Path visualization data flows through the path visualization API polling input configured in UC-5.9.5. No separate input is needed.

Verify path data exists for cloud-targeted tests:
```spl
index=thousandeyes_pathvis sourcetype="cisco:thousandeyes:path-vis" (thousandeyes.test.name="*AWS*" OR thousandeyes.test.name="*Azure*" OR thousandeyes.test.name="*GCP*") earliest=-24h
| stats count dc(thousandeyes.source.agent.name) as agents dc(path_hash) as unique_paths by thousandeyes.test.name
| sort thousandeyes.test.name
```
Each cloud-targeted test should show data. If `unique_paths` is 1 for all tests, the paths are stable (no changes in 24 hours). If >1, paths have changed — which is what this UC investigates.

**Path data vs metric data — key differences:**
- Path data arrives via API polling (every 5–15 minutes), not OTel HEC push. Latency in data availability is 5–15 minutes after the test round completes.
- Path data uses the `path_viz_index` macro (resolves to `index=thousandeyes_pathvis`), NOT the `stream_index` macro.
- Path data provides hop-by-hop detail that metric data doesn't have — you can see exactly which router/AS each packet traverses.

Step 2 — Create the search and alert
**Cloud path stability (primary view — detects path changes):**
```spl
`path_viz_index` thousandeyes.test.name="*AWS*" OR thousandeyes.test.name="*Azure*" OR thousandeyes.test.name="*GCP*"
| stats avg(hop_count) as avg_hops max(hop_count) as max_hops dc(path_hash) as unique_paths by thousandeyes.source.agent.name, server.address
| where unique_paths > 1
| sort -unique_paths
```

**Understanding this SPL**

`dc(path_hash) as unique_paths` — counts the number of distinct network paths observed during the search window. A value of 1 means stable path (same route every time). A value of 2 means the path changed once. A value of >5 means the path is highly unstable — traffic is being load-balanced across multiple routes or BGP is flapping.

`avg(hop_count)` and `max(hop_count)` — average and maximum number of hops to the destination. If `max_hops` >> `avg_hops`, some path variants are much longer (more hops = more latency). A cloud path via private peering might be 8 hops, while a path via public internet might be 15 hops.

`where unique_paths > 1` — only shows tests where path changes occurred. Tests with stable paths are excluded from the alert view.

**Cloud path stability trending** (reveals temporal patterns):
```spl
`path_viz_index` (thousandeyes.test.name="*AWS*" OR thousandeyes.test.name="*Azure*" OR thousandeyes.test.name="*GCP*") earliest=-7d
| timechart span=4h dc(path_hash) as unique_paths by thousandeyes.test.name
```
This shows how many unique paths were observed per 4-hour window over 7 days. Spikes indicate path instability events. Persistent multiple paths indicate BGP multi-path or load-balanced routing.

**Per-hop latency analysis (identifies the slow hop):**
```spl
`path_viz_index` thousandeyes.test.name="$test_name$" earliest=-4h
| sort _time
| stats avg(hop_latency) as avg_hop_latency max(hop_latency) as max_hop_latency by hop_number, hop_ip, hop_prefix
| eval degraded=if(avg_hop_latency > 50 OR max_hop_latency > 200, "YES", "no")
| sort hop_number
```
This shows each hop in order from source to destination, with latency per hop. The hop where latency jumps significantly is the bottleneck. If hop 5 shows 10 ms but hop 6 shows 80 ms, the link between hop 5 and hop 6 is the problem — and the `hop_prefix` for hop 6 tells you which network (ASN) owns that link.

**Cloud ingress point identification:**
```spl
`path_viz_index` thousandeyes.test.name="*AWS*" earliest=-1h
| stats values(hop_ip) as hops values(hop_prefix) as prefixes by thousandeyes.source.agent.name, path_hash
```
Look for where the path transitions from ISP/transit prefixes to cloud provider prefixes (AWS uses AS16509, Azure uses AS8075, GCP uses AS15169). The hop before this transition is the peering point — if that hop shows high latency, the peering interconnect is congested.

**Scheduling:** cron `0 */4 * * *` (every 4 hours), time range `-4h to now`. Trigger when `unique_paths > 3` for any cloud test (frequent path changes suggest BGP instability). Throttle by `thousandeyes.test.name` for 8 hours.

Step 3 — Validate
(a) **Cross-reference ThousandEyes UI.** Navigate to **Cloud & Enterprise Agents → Views → Path Visualization** and select a cloud-targeted test. The UI shows a visual path diagram with colour-coded hops. Compare the hop sequence and per-hop latency with what Splunk reports.

(b) **Traceroute comparison.** From the same network as the Enterprise Agent, run `traceroute` (Linux) or `tracert` (Windows) to the same cloud target. The hop IPs should be similar to what ThousandEyes shows. Note: ThousandEyes uses more sophisticated path detection (multiple protocols, PMTUD) — simple traceroute may show fewer hops if intermediate routers don't respond to ICMP.

(c) **Verify path_hash consistency.** For a known stable path, `dc(path_hash)` should be 1 over a 24-hour window. If it shows >1 but you believe the path is stable, the `path_hash` calculation may include minor variations (ECMP load-balanced hops). Check whether the "changed" hops are all within the same AS/prefix.

(d) **Cloud IP range validation.** For a specific test, extract the destination IP: `| stats values(server.address) by thousandeyes.test.name`. Verify this IP belongs to the expected cloud provider by checking against published IP ranges.

(e) **Data freshness.** Path viz data is polled, not pushed. Check `| stats latest(_time) as most_recent | eval age_min=round((now()-most_recent)/60,0)`. If `age_min` > 30, the polling input may be delayed or failing.

Step 4 — Operationalize
**Dashboard** ("Cloud Network Path Health" — designed for cloud networking team):
• Row 1 — Single value tiles: "Cloud paths with changes (24h)" (red ≥ 1), "Average hops to AWS / Azure / GCP" (green reference for expected), "Path instability events this week".
• Row 2 — Path stability timechart: unique paths per cloud test over 7 days. Spikes represent path change events. Overlay with network latency from UC-5.9.31 to correlate path changes with performance impact.
• Row 3 — Path change detail table: test name | source agent | destination | unique paths | avg hops | max hops | current path hash — sorted by unique paths descending. Drilldown to per-hop analysis for the selected test.
• Row 4 — Per-hop latency waterfall (drill-down): hop number | hop IP | hop prefix (AS) | avg latency | degraded flag. Shows the entire path from source to cloud destination with colour-coded latency.

**Alerting:**
• `unique_paths > 3` for any cloud test in 4 hours → low-urgency notification to `#cloud-networking` (Slack/Teams). Path instability may be transient.
• Path change accompanied by latency increase > 20% (correlate with UC-5.9.31) → high-urgency notification. A path change that degrades performance requires investigation.
• Cloud path changes affecting multiple source sites simultaneously → major peering event. Escalate to ISP/transit team.

**Runbook** (owner: cloud networking / infrastructure team):
1. **Path change detected.** Check per-hop analysis. Identify which hop changed and which network (ASN) is involved.
2. **ISP peering change.** If the new path goes through a different ISP or transit provider, check whether latency increased (UC-5.9.31). If yes, contact the ISP about the peering change. Many ISPs make "cold potato" routing changes to save costs, which increases latency for customers.
3. **Cloud provider routing change.** If the path change is within the cloud provider's network (same cloud AS but different internal path), check the cloud provider's status page. Cloud providers occasionally re-route traffic during maintenance or to mitigate internal congestion.
4. **Path instability (frequent changes).** If `unique_paths` > 5 in a few hours, BGP is likely flapping. Check BGP metrics (UC-5.9.8-5.9.11) for the same prefix. BGP flapping can cause packet loss and latency spikes.
5. **Evaluate dedicated interconnect.** If paths to a specific cloud provider are consistently unstable or high-latency, consider a dedicated interconnect: AWS Direct Connect, Azure ExpressRoute, or GCP Cloud Interconnect. These provide private, stable paths that bypass the public internet entirely.

Step 5 — Troubleshooting

• **No path visualization data** — The path visualization API polling input may not be configured. Check **Inputs → Path Visualization** in the app. Also verify OAuth token has the correct permissions (path viz requires read access to the test results API).

• **Path data exists but `hop_count`, `hop_ip` fields are missing** — The field names may differ depending on how the app parses path viz API responses. Run `| fieldsummary | search field=hop*` to identify the actual field names.

• **All tests show `unique_paths=1`** — Paths are stable. This is good! The UC is working correctly — it only alerts when changes occur. If you expect to see changes (e.g., during an ISP maintenance window), re-run the search for that specific time window.

• **`path_viz_index` macro not defined** — Check Settings → Advanced Search → Search Macros. If the macro doesn't exist, create it: name `path_viz_index`, definition `index=thousandeyes_pathvis`.

• **Data is stale (latest event is hours old)** — The API polling input may have stopped. Check `index=_internal "thousandeyes" "path" ERROR` for API errors (OAuth token expired, rate limiting, network connectivity to ThousandEyes API).

• **All common troubleshooting** — See UC-5.9.1 Step 5 for OAuth refresh, macro configuration, and general app troubleshooting. See UC-5.9.5 Step 5 for path-visualization-specific issues.

**IPv6 Note:** ICMPv6 is architecturally critical for IPv6 — it carries NDP (Neighbor Discovery), Path MTU Discovery, and Multicast Listener Discovery. Unlike ICMP for IPv4, blocking ICMPv6 breaks IPv6 connectivity entirely. Ensure firewall policies permit at minimum ICMPv6 types 1-4 (Destination Unreachable, Packet Too Big, Time Exceeded, Parameter Problem) and types 133-137 (RS, RA, NS, NA, Redirect). See RFC 4890 for filtering recommendations.
grandmaExplanation: We track the exact route our internet traffic takes to reach our cloud services, because if the route suddenly changes and goes through a longer or congested path, everything in the cloud gets slower.
